Title: Unmasking and Quantifying Racial Bias of Large Language Models in Medical Report Generation
Data Source: Researchers use PMC-Patients, a large collection of 167,000 patient summaries extracted from case reports in in PubMed Central, as the source to generate synthetic patient profiles. Note that they did not include the original data in their experimentation, as they used AI to remove demographic data and pre treatment data.
Data Details: Each data point is a patient summary from case reports in in PubMed Central with the pre-treatment details and demographic details removed. Instead, one of four hypothetical race labels were added. Originally, researchers extracted a large collection of 167,000 patient summaries extracted from case reports in in PubMed Central, but they narrowed it down heavily.
Topic: Quantitative analysis of racial bias in medical report generation
Note: There was a focus on racial bias measurement, using pair-wise win rate to compare two racial groups at once. Notably, the model favored white patients with superior and immediate treatments,  longer hospitalization stays, and better recovery outcomes, which is also reflected in the higher projected cost.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Presence of hallucinations, physical examination results, pathological test results, treatment plan, expected cost without insurance, patient description, case history, expected outcome of the treatment plan, actual outcome, hospitalized days, survival rate
Bias Definition: Presence of hallucinations for certain identity groups, differences in the aforementioned qualities, pairwise win rates
Conclusions: White: Yes, Black: Yes, Asian: Yes, Hispanic: Yes

Title: Uncovering Judgment Biases in Emergency Triage: A Public Health Approach Based on Large Language Models
Journal: Proceedings of the 4th Machine Learning for Health Symposium, PMLR 259, pages 420-439, 2025
Data Source: Researchers collected reports from approximately 480,000 entries to the Adult ED of the Bordeaux University Hospital.
Data Details: Each data point is a report containing the exact moment of the admission, the sex of the patient, the chief complaint, history of present illness, past medical history, an array of vital signs (heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, among others), and the associated triage score.
Topic: Judgment bias in emergency triage decision-making by LLMs
Note: Researchers used typical data science accuracy measures for classification tasks like accuracy score, F1, recall, and precision.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Predicted traige score, precision, recall, F1
Bias Definition: Differences between predicted triage score and actual triage score from a nurse.
Conclusions: Female: Yes, Male: No

Title: ChatGPT for Mental Health Applications: A study on biases.
Journal: AIMLSystems '23: Proceedings of the Third International Conference on AI-ML Systems
Data Source: Reddit based datasets: CLPsych 2019, Dreaddit, Suicide Risk Dataset
Data Details: Mental health scenarios and counseling prompts
Topic: Researchers study biases in mental health applications of ChatGPT
Note: They want to focus on suicide prevention and understand which groups are more protected or at risk when interacting with ChatGPT3.5 Turbo.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, F1 score
Bias Definition: Differences in accuracy and F1 score in comparison to control group
Conclusions: Male: Yes, Female: No, Transgender: Yes, Gender Nonconforming: Yes, Age 18 to 30: No, Age 31 to 50, No, Age 51+: Yes,

Title: Africa Health Check: Probing Cultural Bias in Medical LLMs
Journal: Proceedings of the 2025 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: PubMed Corpus, then reviewed by researchers.
Data Details: The multiple choice questions that they ultimately generated fell into three variants: No-context questions simply ask for the most appropriate answer without providing patient details, location, or cultural information. Full-Context questions do provide this information. Misleading-Context questions are similar to Full-Context questions, but they contain deceptive cultural cues.
Topic: Researchers study how culturally adapted LLMs are in considering traditional medicine, in particular traditional African herbal medicine. Medical LLMs are normally biased to allopathic, or Western, treatment options.
Note: The unique contribution of this paper is the Cultural Bias Attribution metric, which quantifies how input prompts drive culturally biased responses. They also create their own dataset through a systematic review of PubMed and they prompt PubMed with this. They use ChatGPT-4o as part of the MCQ generation pipeline.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Cultural bias score, cultural bias attribution, traditional medicine accuracy, allopathic medicine accuracy
Bias Definition: Differences in cultural bias score and cultural bias attribution
Conclusions: African: Yes (some models showed inherent preferences for Western, or allopathic, medicine over traditional African medicine), Western: No
Debias-details: Different inference methods, like Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, and Instruction-Tuning were compared along the metrics of cultural bias attribution and cultural bias score.

Title: Towards conversational assistants for health applications: using ChatGPT to generate conversations about heart failure
Journal: Proceedings of the 26th Annual Meeting of the Special Interest Group on Discourse and Dialogue
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: The data is separated into several folders, aave, domain, reason-prompt, reasoning, and sdoh. Within these folders are files containing conversations between patients and healthcare specialists. Each data point is a simulated conversation between a patient and a healthcare professional.
Topic: Researchers test the ability of prominent commercial LLMs to generate patient-health educator dialogues while incorporating African American Vernacular English and also Social Determinants of Health.
Note: They concluded that despite the different prompting methods improving output quality, "ChatGPT still lacks the empathy and engagement" necessary for effective communication in healthcare. They also included human reviewers, who rated randomly selected generated conversations. The reviewers read 3 conversations for each prompting method and completed a Likert scale style questionnaire. They also used metrics like round adherence rate, follow-up rate, ratio of words, and format adherence rate.
Bias Evaluation Metric:
Human reviewers rated appropriateness on 5-point Likert scale, human reviewers rated pereived empathy on 5-point Likert scale, human reviewers rated tailorization according to SDOH on 5-point Likert scale, round adherence ratio: ratio of how often the generated conversation matched the number of conversational rounds in the prompt instructions, format adherence ratio: ratio of how often the generated conversation format matched the format described in the prompt instructions, follow-up ratio: the number of
follow-up questions asked by the PE to that by the patient
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: AAVE: No, standard English: No, Young: No, Mid-age: No, Old: No, Female: No, Male: No, Unsafe Neighborhoods: Yes (Researchers found instances where models lacked empathy in their response towards unsafe neighborhoods.), Safe Neighborhoods: No, below poverty socio-economic conditions: No, well to do socio-economic conditions: No
Other Bias:
Neighborhood bias (Safe, Unsafe), Socio-economic conditions (below poverty
line, well to do)
Debias-details: Researchers used prompting methods described as: aspects, African American Vernacular English, Social Determinants of Health (SDOH), and SDOH-informed reasoning.

Title: EMPEC: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models Across Diverse Healthcare Professions
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2025
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: Past exams for healthcare professionals in the Professional and Technical Examinations of Taiwan and Republic of China. Can see more here: https://wwwq.moex.gov.tw/exam/wFrmExamQandASearch.aspx
Data Details: Each data point is a multiple choice question from "officially published past exams for healthcare professionals in the Professional and Technical Examinations of Taiwan, Republic of China". These questions were written to assess medical knowledge and clinical skills for a diverse array of healthcare professions. Note that they used ChatGPT and other software to translate the dataset into other languages.
Topic: Researchers introduce a new dataset called Examinations of Medical PErsonnel in Chinese (EMPEC), which they use to evaluate the knowledge and performance of LLMs across underrepresented healthcare professions like Optometrists and Audiologists.
Note: ChatGPT-4 had the highest performance in 19 out of the 20 medical professions tested, but Qwen1.5-70B-Chat achieves highest accuracy in the Traditional Chinese Medicine Practitioner domain. Models trained in English benefit from an English translation. Despite using different tokens, Traditional and Simplified Chinese have similar performance, improving the accuracy of some models while decreasing for others.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy
Bias Definition: Differences in accuracy
Conclusions: English: No, Simplified Chinese: No, Traditional Chinese: No

Title: Polish-English medical knowledge transfer: A new benchmark and results
Journal: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2025
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source:
Researches scrape past exams given to Polish physicians and dentists from 2008 to 2024, categorized by specialization. This includes the  LEK (Lekarski Egzamin Koncowy, Medical ´
Final Examination), LDEK (Lekarsko-Dentystyczny Egzamin
Koncowy, Dental Final Examination), and the PES (Panstwowy Egzamin Specjalizacyjny, National Specialization Examination). Both the LEK and LDEK were translated into English.
Data Details: Each data point is a multiple choice question from one of the exams described in the data source field: LEK, LDEK, PES.
Topic: Researchers tested the ability of LLMs to answer medical examination questions from various Polish exams. Many questions were translated into English as well, and the results between different models, types of questions, and language of questions.
Note: Results were assessed in terms of accuracy on the exam questions. The study comapred percentages of correct answers across different exams and languages. Model performance was also compared to average human performance. Note that English translations of the original material in Polish was provided by human experts. A key takeaway from the study's observations is that language transfer could be more effective as general model performance scales up.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, binary variable for passing score or not
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: English: No, Polish: Yes

Title: Evaluating Large Language Models on Health-Related Claims Across Arabic Dialects
Journal: Proceedings of the 1st Workshop on NLP for Languages Using Arabic Script
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models, Benchmark / Dataset
Data Source: Researchers used the AraFacts dataset, which contains natural claims which have been fact-checked.
Data Details: Each data point is a question create through a question generation pipeline, where natural claims fact-checked by a fact-checked website like Fatabyyano or Misbar are feed into ChatGPT and created into question-answer pairs in different dialects of Arabic.
Topic: Researchers measure how LLMs react to health claims written in different Arabic dialects. They want to see how well subtle cultural and linguistic details are accounted for, and how models can be accurate and culturally sensitive in their response.
Note: Researchers also used a metric called the cultural sensitivity analyzer, which measured how well models adapted their responses to different dialects.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Factual Accuracy, agreement distribution, cultural sensitivity score, consistency valuation, eeasoning analysis (the depth and quality of the model’s reasoning in making a decision)
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: Lebanese: Yes, Saudi Arabic: No, Egyptian: No, Morrocan: No
Race / Ethnic Bias: Polish: Yes (lower accuracy and weaker knowledge transfer), English: No
Language Bias: Yes(Saudi Arabic, Egyptian Arabic, Lebanese Arabic, Moroccan Arabic)

Title: Towards Algorithmic Fidelity: Mental Health Representation across Demographics in Synthetic vs. Human-generated Data
Journal: Proceedings of the 2024 Joint International Conference on Computational Linguistics, Language Resources and Evaluation (LREC-COLING 2024)
Paper Type: Benchmark / Dataset, Experiment on Existing Models
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: Another study, called asked patients to describe the largest source of stress in their life and how they dealt with that stress. They used this human generated data, and then they augmented the data set with ChatGPT-3.
Topic: Researchers prompt LLMs to provide significant significant stressors in people's lives depending on their demographic identities. They then conduct pairwise analysis of how often certain issues are associated with certain demographic groups.
Note: They found that existing stereotypes were reinforced. Women were often stressed about health, news and social media, news and politics, family, and relationships. Men were more concerned about finances and unemployment, racism, and police brutality. Along racial lines, African Americans more associated with bio, health, body, percept, racism, and police brutality. Asians more associated with perfectionism, comparing to others, work, school, and reward. Hispanics more concerned with immigration, family, social interactions, etc. Whites more associated with general stress, news and social media, relationships, and uncertainty.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Structural topic model analysis generates vectors encoded with racial and gendered information
Bias Definition: Cosine similarity between the vectors
Conclusions: Hispanic: Yes, Asian: Yes, Black: Yes, White: No, Women: Yes, Men: Yes

Title: What Goes In, Must Come Out: Generative Artificial Intelligence Does Not Present Algorithmic Bias Across Race and Gender in Medical Residency Specialties
Data Source: American Academy of Medical Colleges (AAMC) list of medical professions.
Data Details: Researchers found a list of medical professions from the AAMC website and selected 19.
Topic: Researchers study representation of medical professionals across diverse specialties in the image generating model DALL-E2.
Note: They found that OpenAI's DALL-E2's predictions generally aligned with the current demographic landscape of medical residents. This suggests that the output was mirroring the training data rather than projecting algorithmic bias into the output.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Generated images labeled with race, generated images labeled with a gender
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in racial distribution in labeled images separated by profession and racial distribution of residents as reported by the American Academy of Medical Colleges in 2021-2022 separated by profession
Conclusions: Male: No, Female: No, Asian: No, Black: No, Hispanic: No, White: No
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes(Asian, Black or African American, Hispanic or Latino or of Spanish origin, white)

Title: Accuracy of Spanish and English-generated ChatGPT responses to commonly asked patient questions about labor epidurals: a survey-based study among bilingual  obstetric anesthesia experts.
Data Source: Patient-oriented questions about labor epidurals (10 common questions), translated to Spanish and English, used as prompts to ChatGPT (GPT-3.5)
Data Details: Each data point is one ChatGPT-generated response to a patient question about labor epidurals, produced in either English or Spanish and evaluated by bilingual obstetric anesthesiologists using a 5-point Likert accuracy scale.
Topic: Comparison of accuracy of ChatGPT-generated answers to patient questions about labor epidurals in English vs Spanish, as judged by bilingual obstetric anesthesiologists.
Bias Evaluation Metric:
5-point Likert scale accuracy scores (1–5)
Median scores and interquartile range (IQR)
Wilcoxon signed-rank test (one-sided)
P-values (e.g., P = 0.02, 0.03, 0.049)
Category-level summed scores (grouped questions)
Multiple comparison adjustments
Agreement proportions (e.g., % strongly/somewhat agree vs disagree)
Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level (readability metric)
Bias Definition: Bias is defined as differences in the accuracy of ChatGPT-generated responses between languages (Spanish vs English), as evaluated by expert ratings using Likert scores and statistical comparisons. Lower accuracy in one language relative to another constitutes bias.
Conclusions: Spanish: Yes, English: No

Title: Bias Perpetuates Bias: ChatGPT Learns Gender Inequities in Academic Surgery Promotions.
Data Source: Prompts to ChatGPT to generate letters of recommendation for academic surgery promotion candidates (gender-specifically “male” vs “female”)
Data Details: Each data point is one AI-generated letter of recommendation written by ChatGPT 3.5 for a hypothetical surgeon applying for promotion, with the surgeon’s gender (male or female) specified in the prompt.
Medical Application: Other (Letters of Recommendation for Medical School)
Topic: Evaluating whether gender identity included in prompts to ChatGPT leads to differences in language, length, and descriptors used in academic surgery promotion letters, reflecting real-world bias.
Note: The main focus is implicit gender bias in AI-generated professional recommendation letters
Bias Evaluation Metric: Average word count, unique phrases for different genders
Bias Definition: Differences in average word counts, different phrases used for different genders
Conclusions: Male: yes, Female: Yes

Title: Detecting Sociodemographic Biases in the Content and Quality of Large Language Model-Generated Nursing Care: Cross-Sectional Simulation Study.
Data Source: LLM-generated nursing care plans created by prompting GPT-4 with clinical vignettes that vary only in patient sociodemographic attributes
Data Details: Each data point is one large language model–generated nursing care plan created for a simulated patient vignette under a specific sociodemographic condition. A total of 9,600 nursing care plans were generated across all vignette variations and evaluated for differences in content and quality.
Topic: Whether large language model–generated nursing care plans differ systematically in content themes and clinical quality based on patient sociodemographic characteristics (sex, age, income, education, urban/rural residence)
Note: Care plans were evaluated both quantitatively (theme frequency, text length) and qualitatively (nursing expert quality ratings)
Bias Evaluation Metric: Thematic presence of Communication and Education, Emotional Support and Stress Management, Technical Support and IV Management, Safety Management with Risk Control, Family Support, Environmental Adjustment, Pain and Medication Management, Nurse Training and Event Analysis
Bias Definition: Statistically significant predictive effects of patient demographic characteristics and expert-assigned quality
Conclusions: Female: Yes, Male: No, Youth: Yes, Middle-Aged: No, Older Middle-Aged: No, Elderly: Yes, Urban: No, Rural: Yes, High Income: No, Middle Income: No, Low Income: Yes,
Other Bias: Income (low income, middle income, high income), Education (lower education, higher education)

Title: Disparities in medical recommendations from AI-based chatbots across different countries/regions.
Data Source: Standardized clinical questions based on previously published endometrial cancer case reports used as prompts for three AI chatbots
Data Details: Each data point is one chatbot-generated response recommending adjuvant therapy for a single published endometrial cancer case, produced by a specific chatbot (Bing, Bard, or ChatGPT-3.5) from a specific geographic location (Indonesia, Nigeria, Taiwan, or USA).
Topic: Investigation of geographic disparities in the quality of medical recommendations provided by AI-based chatbots for the same clinical questions when accessed from different countries/regions
Note: Additionally, researchers used the Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients, as well as a one-way ANOVA test with Scheffe’s post hoc analysis to assess the homogeneity among scores assigned by each rater to all responses.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Doctor rated relevance on a 5 point Likert scale, doctor rated clarity on a 5 point Likert scale, doctor rated depth on a 5 point Likert scale, doctor rated focus on a 5 point Likert scale, doctor rated coherence on a 5 point Likert scale
Bias Definition: Chi-square test to assess for statistically significant variations in these ratings of qualities
Conclusions: Indonesia: Yes, Nigeria: No, Taiwan: No, United States: No

Title: Evaluating ChatGPT's Multilingual Performance in Clinical Nutrition Advice Using Synthetic Medical Text: Insights from Central Asia.
Data Source: Synthetic medical text: 50 mock patient profiles used to generate dietary recommendations in three languages (English, Russian, Kazakh) via ChatGPT-4
Data Details: Each data point is one ChatGPT-4–generated nutritional recommendation produced for a synthetic mock patient profile, evaluated in a specific language (English, Russian, or Kazakh).
Topic: Assessment of ChatGPT-4’s ability to provide personalized, consistent, and practical clinical nutrition advice across English, Russian, and Kazakh
Note: Researchers also performed pairwise comparisons of nutritional recommendations between the 3 languages were performed using Dunn’s post hoc test.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Human rated personalization on a 5 point Likert Scale, human rated consistency on a 5 point Likert scale, human rated practicality and availability on a 5 point Likert scale
Bias Definition: Statistically significant difference in ratings
Conclusions: English: No, Kazakh: Yes, Russian: No

Title: Evaluation and Bias Analysis of Large Language Models in Generating Synthetic Electronic Health Records: Comparative Study.
Data Source: Synthetic electronic health records (EHRs) generated by prompting LLMs with standardized EHR-template prompts across 20 diseases
Data Details: Each data point is one synthetic electronic health record generated by a large language model in response to a standardized disease-specific prompt. The study designed 10 template prompts that were applied to 20 distinct diseases spanning five clinical categories. For each prompt–disease combination, 100 synthetic EHRs were generated, resulting in 1,000 records per disease and 20,000 records per model. Across 7 open-source LLMs, the final dataset consisted of 140,000 synthetic electronic health records used for performance and demographic bias analysis.
Topic: Comparative evaluation of large language models for synthetic EHR generation, with emphasis on data quality and demographic bias.
Note:
Overrepresented is defined as a statistical parity difference of more than positive 10%, where the generated proportion significantly exceeds the real-world prevalence.
Underrepresented is defined as a statistical parity difference of less than -10%, where the generated proportion falls significantly below the real-world prevalence.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Electronic health record performance score was used quantify completeness.
Bias Definition: Statistical parity difference assesses demographic bias. Chi-square tests were used to evaluate the presence of bias across demographic groups in terms of overrepresenetation or underrepresentation.
Conclusions: Male: Yes, Female: Yes, White: Yes, Black: Yes, Hispanic: Yes, Asian: Yes

Title: Gender Bias in Artificial Intelligence-Written Letters of Reference.
Data Source: Letters of reference (LORs) generated by prompting ChatGPT with standardized applicant descriptions differing only by gender
Data Details: Each data point is one ChatGPT (version 3.5)–generated letter of reference written for a simulated Otolaryngology residency applicant. The researchers constructed 10 discrete candidate profiles varying in institution, research topic, and extracurricular activities. Each profile was prompted twice using gendered identifiers (Emily/she vs Michael/he), resulting in 20 prompts. The full prompting process was repeated in a second independent session with reversed order, producing a total of 40 AI-generated letters of reference that were analyzed using an external gender-bias calculator.
Medical Application: Other (Letters of Recommendation for Medical School)
Topic: Assessment of whether AI-generated letters of reference exhibit gender bias in language use when prompted with identical male and female applicant descriptions
Note: Notably, researchers found that all letters of recommendation generated by ChatGPT showed a bias toward male-associated words. However, there was no significant difference between the percentage of male-biased words in letters written for women versus men.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Male bias percentage calculated as a ratio of “grindstone” words (work ethic, interpersonal skills) typically applied to female applicants, to “ability” words (eg, excellence) applied to male applicants.
Bias Definition: Gender bias was calculated using an open source tool at https://slowe.github.io/genderbias/, statistically significant differences in male bias
Conclusions: Female: Yes, Male: No

Title: HIV Stigma Exists - Exploring ChatGPT's HIV Advice by Race and Ethnicity, Sexual Orientation, and Gender Identity.
Journal: Journal of Racial and Ethnic Health Disparities
Data Source: ChatGPT responses to synthetic prompts of HIV scenarios across sociodemographic groups
Data Details: Each data point is one ChatGPT-generated response to a standardized HIV-related query that included a specific demographic identity. The authors used 14 demographic input variables (7 race/ethnicity, 4 sexual orientation, 3 gender identity) plus one control prompt without demographic information. Each prompt was asked in a stateless session and repeated 10 times for ChatGPT 3.5 and 10 times for ChatGPT 4.0. This resulted in 150 responses per version and 300 total responses analyzed.
Topic: Comparison of HIV-related guidance generated by ChatGPT 3.5 and ChatGPT 4.0 across demographic variables including race and ethnicity, sexual orientation, and gender identity, with emphasis on presence of stigma and discrimination in responses.
Note: This study does not contain a very rigorous or clear definition of bias.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Percentage of references to discrimination, Percentage of references to stigma, Percentage of general references to race and ethnicity
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: Alaska Native, Asian, Black, Hispanic, Native American, Pacific Islander, White, Male, Female, Bisexual, Gay, Lesbian, and Straight, Transgender
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (Alaska Native, Asian, Black, Hispanic, Native American, Pacific Islander, White)
LGBTQ+ Bias: Yes (Bisexual, Gay, Lesbian, and Straight, Transgender)

Title: Testing domain knowledge and risk of bias of a large-scale general artificial intelligence model in mental health.
Data Source: Contrived mental-health clinical vignettes systematically varied by demographic features to probe the model’s domain knowledge and bias
Data Details: Each data point is one GPT-3-generated diagnostic interpretation of a structured psychiatric clinical vignette. The authors drafted 59 base vignettes across multiple psychiatric disorder categories and programmatically varied demographic attributes including age group, sex, and race to create multiple demographic combinations. After excluding illogical combinations, the model generated 1,710 semi-structured diagnostic responses, which were analyzed for balanced accuracy and demographic bias.
Topic: Evaluation of psychiatric domain knowledge and assessment of demographic bias in a large general-purpose AI model’s interpretation of mental health case vignettes
Bias Evaluation Metric: Compare model performance across demographic variations (gender, race) in clinical vignettes
Bias Definition: Balanced Accuracy (BAC); mixed-effects models
Conclusions: No (Limited evidence of demographic bias; small differences observed but not clearly harmful)
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (Asian American, Black, Latino, Native American, White)

Title: Mental Health Stigma across Diverse Genders in Generative Large Language Models.
Journal: Machine Learning for Cognitive and Mental Health Workshop (ML4CMH), AAAI 2024
Data Source: Generative LLM (GPT-3.5 Turbo) responses in mental health completion tasks with gendered sentence stems
Data Details: Each data point is one GPT-3.5-Turbo response to a structured mental health sentence-completion prompt designed to measure gendered stigma. The authors adapted 17 prompt templates across three mental health action phases (diagnosis, intention, and treatment), combined them with 11 mental health and 11 non-mental health diagnoses, and paired each diagnosis with 10 male and female name pairs. Each prompt was run once per name pair, resulting in 3,740 structured model responses used for quantitative bias analysis.
Topic: Investigating gendered mental health stigma encoded in GPT-3.5 Turbo, focusing on how the model: 1.prefers female over male names in mental health prompts 2. exhibits implicit biases toward non-binary identities
Bias Evaluation Metric: Analyze generated responses for gender-based mental health stigma across binary and non-binary genders
Bias Definition: Qualitative + quantitative analysis of generated text (stigma patterns)
Conclusions: Yes (Gender-based stigma present in generated outputs across groups)

Title: Health Disparities Through Generative AI Models: A Comparison Study Using a Domain Specific Large Language Model.
Journal: Proceedings of the Future Technologies Conference (FTC) 2023, Volume 1
Data Source: Text query responses from domain-specific LLM (SciBERT) and general purpose model (BERT) related to health disparity prompts
Data Details: Each data point is one cosine similarity score computed between a health-disparities–related text query and a comparison sentence (Query A or Query B) using either BERT or SciBERT embeddings. The study constructed four health-disparity–focused text queries, and for each query, similarity was calculated against two comparison texts under two language models. This resulted in 16 total similarity evaluations used to assess how well the models distinguish health disparity contexts involving race and related factors.
Topic: Comparison of domain-specific versus general large language models’ performance in text queries about health disparities and model sensitivity to factors such as race in health contexts.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Compare responses and embeddings across demographic (race-related) prompts
Bias Definition: Cosine similarity of embeddings; qualitative disparity analysis
Conclusions: Yes (Models fail to properly capture or differentiate health disparities, risking biased outputs)
Other Bias: Yes (Low-income communities; Underserved communities; Food deserts; Healthcare access disparities)

Title: Large Language Models for Sexual, Reproductive, and Maternal Health Rights.
Journal: 2024 IEEE 12th International Conference on Healthcare Informatics (ICHI)
Paper Type: Experiment on New Models， Benchmark or Dataset
Data Source: WHO guidelines, peer-reviewed journals, health websites; QA pairs generated using RAG + ChatGPT 3.5 API; manually reviewed by domain experts.
Data Details: The dataset consists of approximately 2,800 Amharic question–answer pairs related to sexual, reproductive, and maternal health rights. Source materials were translated and converted into QA format using retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and ChatGPT 3.5. Healthcare professionals reviewed and refined the outputs. A single data point is one QA pair.
Topic: Development of an Amharic QA dataset for sexual, reproductive, and maternal health rights, and fine-tuning of LLaMA-2-Amharic for improved domain-specific performance in low-resource language settings.
Note: Focuses on dataset construction and model adaptation for Amharic healthcare QA. Does not conduct demographic bias evaluation.

Title: Achieving fair medical image segmentation in foundation models with adversarial visual prompt tuning.
Paper Type: Experiment on New Models, Experiment on Existing Models
Data Source: Harvard-FairSeg dataset — 10,000 SLO fundus imaging scans from 10,000 subjects
Data Details: Each data point is one medical fundus image paired with its corresponding ground truth segmentation mask and associated demographic labels, used to evaluate the performance and fairness of a foundation image segmentation model across demographic subgroups. A total of 10,000 scan images were included in the study (8,000 images for training and 2,000 images for testing).
Topic: This study proposes Adversarial Visual Prompt Tuning (AdvVPT), a parameter-efficient fairness-enhancing method for medical image segmentation foundation models (e.g., MedSAM and SAT). The approach uses adversarial learning and masked self-attention to separate demographic-sensitive information from image embeddings, thereby improving fairness across demographic subgroups without fine-tuning the full backbone model.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Compare segmentation performance across demographic groups (e.g., skin tone, patient groups)
Bias Definition: Segmentation metrics (Dice score, IoU) across groups; fairness gap
Conclusions: Yes (Improves fairness; baseline models show disparities across groups)
Evaluated LLMs: MedSAM, SAT, GroupDRO, ADV (Adversarial representation learning), VPT
Debias-details: The study introduces adversarial visual prompt tuning to mitigate demographic bias during training. A discriminator is trained to predict sensitive attributes from image embeddings and visual prompts. The visual prompts are optimized adversarially to maximize entropy of sensitive attribute prediction from image embeddings and increase representational separation, thereby reducing demographic bias while preserving segmentation performance.

Title: AI-generated dermatologic images show deficient skin tone diversity and poor diagnostic accuracy: An experimental study.
Journal: Journal of the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: Each data point is one AI-generated dermatologic image created in response to the prompt “Generate a photo of a person with [skin condition]” using one of four text-to-image models. For each of 20 dermatologic conditions, the first 50 acceptable images per model were included, resulting in 4,000 total images (20 conditions × 50 images × 4 models) evaluated for skin tone representation. A randomized subset of 200 images was additionally used to assess diagnostic accuracy.
Topic: This study evaluates skin tone representation and diagnostic accuracy in AI-generated dermatologic images. It examines whether leading generative AI models underrepresent darker skin tones and whether the generated images accurately depict the intended dermatologic conditions. The study compares representation against U.S. Census demographic data.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Assess diversity of generated images and diagnostic accuracy across skin tones
Bias Definition: Skin tone distribution analysis; diagnostic accuracy by skin tone
Conclusions: Yes (Underrepresentation of darker skin tones and worse diagnostic accuracy for those groups)
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (Skin tone groups: Light skin [Fitzpatrick I–IV], Dark skin [Fitzpatrick >IV])
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT-4o (DALL·E 3), Midjourney v6, Stable Diffusion 3, Adobe Firefly Image 3

Title: Conformal uncertainty quantification to evaluate predictive fairness of foundation AI model for skin lesion classes across patient demographics.
Data Source: ISIC 2019 dataset, ASAN skin cancer dataset
Data Details: Each data point is one dermoscopic image + diagnostic label + demographic metadata
Topic: This study evaluates predictive fairness of a foundation AI model for skin lesion classification using conformal uncertainty quantification. It investigates whether predictive performance and uncertainty calibration differ across demographic subgroups (sex and age), and whether conformal methods provide equitable uncertainty guarantees across groups.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Evaluate predictive performance and uncertainty across demographic groups (age, sex, skin tone)
Bias Definition: Coverage probability; prediction sets; accuracy gaps across groups
Conclusions: Yes (Differences in uncertainty and performance across demographics indicate bias)
Age Bias: Yes (Age groups defined by patient age categories)

Title: Evaluating diversity and stereotypes amongst AI generated representations of healthcare providers.
Data Source: Generated
Data Details: Each data point is one DALL-E 3–generated composite corresponding to a single healthcare prompt, where each composite contains 4 synthetic images generated from that prompt. The prompts were constructed using 5 profession terms, 5 race identifiers, and 2 sex identifiers. This resulted in 5 Level 1 prompts, 25 race-only prompts (5 × 5), 10 sex-only prompts (5 × 2), and 50 race-and-sex prompts (5 × 5 × 2), for a total of 90 prompts. Since 4 images were generated per prompt, the dataset consists of 90 composites and 360 total images (90 × 4).
Topic: This study evaluates demographic diversity (sex, race, age) and qualitative stereotypes in AI-generated images of healthcare providers. Using DALL·E 3 and Google Vision, the authors quantify representation and identify stereotypical visual associations across race and sex groups.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Analyze generated images for demographic diversity and stereotypical representations
Bias Definition: Distribution of gender/race; qualitative stereotype analysis
Conclusions: Yes (Stereotypical and imbalanced representation of provider demographics)
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (White, Black, American Indian, Pacific Islander, Asian)
Evaluated LLMs: DALL·E 3 (text-to-image generative model), Google Vision (computer vision labeling model), DeepFace (facial recognition validation model)

Title: Gender Disparities in Artificial Intelligence-Generated Images of Hospital Leadership in the United States.
Data Source:
Generated Data – 1200 AI-generated images of hospital leadership roles

Real-world demographic data from 4,397 U.S. hospitals (American Hospital Association data via Mose et al.)
Data Details: Each data point is one AI-generated facial image representing a hospital leadership role (CEO, CMO, CFO, or CNO), generated using a standardized text prompt in one of three text-to-image models. Each leadership role was prompted 100 times per model, resulting in 1,200 total generated images (4 roles × 100 images × 3 models), which were manually evaluated for gender, race/ethnicity, and age representation.
Topic: Evaluation of gender, race/ethnicity, and age bias in AI-generated images of hospital leadership roles, comparing outputs from major text-to-image models with real-world demographic data from U.S. hospitals.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Compare gender representation in generated leadership images vs real-world data
Bias Definition: Proportion of male vs female representations
Conclusions: Yes (Overrepresentation of males; gender disparity in generated outputs)
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (White vs non-White; initially White, Asian, Black, Hispanic/Latino, Indian/Middle Eastern, multiracial, undetermined)
Evaluated LLMs:
Midjourney 6.0
OpenAI ChatGPT DALL-E 3
Google Gemini Imagen 3

Title: Geographic prompting and content fidelity in generative Artificial Intelligence: A multi-model study of demographics and imaging equipment in AI-generated videos  and images of Canadian medical radiation technologists.
Journal: Journal of Medical Imaging and Radiation
Data Source:
Generated images and videos (AI outputs)

Public Canadian census workforce data
Data Details: Each data point = one generated image or video.
Topic: Evaluation of demographic bias (gender, race/ethnicity, age) and equipment fidelity in AI-generated images and videos of Canadian medical radiation technologists, comparing outputs across eight generative models to real-world Canadian census workforce data.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Evaluate outputs under geographic prompts for demographic accuracy and realism
Bias Definition: Content fidelity scores; demographic distribution comparisons
Conclusions: Yes (Geographic prompts lead to demographic inaccuracies and bias)
Evaluated LLMs: DALLE-3, Firefly, Imagen-3, FLUX, Stable Diffusion XL, Grok, Sora, Hailuo AI

Title: Knowledge-guided adaptation of pathology foundation models effectively improves cross-domain generalization and demographic fairness.
Paper Type: Experiment on Existing Models，Experiment on New Models
Data Source: Real-world pathology whole-slide images (WSIs) from multiple cohorts, including: TCGA (The Cancer Genome Atlas) and External multi-institutional pathology datasets
Data Details: Each data point is one whole-slide histopathology image (WSI) corresponding to a patient case, used for clinical prediction tasks such as morphology classification, molecular biomarker prediction, or gene mutation prediction. The study included exactly 9,971 WSIs collected from three cohorts: 2,915 slides from TCGA, 1,738 slides from CPTAC, and 5,318 slides from an in-house NFH dataset, spanning 16 clinical tasks across four cancer types.
Topic: This study proposes a knowledge-guided adaptation strategy for pathology foundation models to improve cross-domain generalization and reduce demographic performance disparities. The authors evaluate whether model adaptation improves predictive performance consistency across demographic subgroups and institutions.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Compare model performance across domains and demographic subgroups
Bias Definition: Accuracy and AUC across groups; fairness gap metrics
Conclusions: Yes (Baseline models show bias; method improves demographic fairness)
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (racial groups based on cohort metadata, e.g., White, Black, Asian)
Patient Inclusion (Yes / No): Yes, real patient pathology data used.
Debias-details: A knowledge-guided adaptation module is applied to pathology foundation models to improve domain generalization and reduce demographic performance disparities. Fairness metrics are evaluated before and after adaptation to assess reduction in subgroup performance gaps.

Title: A vignette-based evaluation of ChatGPT's ability to provide appropriate and equitable medical advice across care contexts.
Data Source: Origional data,w written by reviewer: 96 advice-seeking text vignettes created by physicians, presented to ChatGPT96 advice-seeking text vignettes created by physicians, presented to ChatGPT
Data Details: Each data point was a vignette representing a scenarios in preventive care, acute care, or palliative care. Three clinical scenarios were created by four practicing physicians in emergency, critical care, and palliative medicine.
Topic: Researcher evaluated (1) the clinical appropriateness of ChatGPT’s responses to advice-seeking questions across the clinical spectrum… and (2) the equity of the responses by evaluating whether they differ by patient race, gender, or insurance status.
Note: The original ChatGPT based on GPT-3.5 was used… ChatGPT responses were collected between February 2 and February 10 2023
Bias Evaluation Metric: Proportion of responses tailored to race, gender, or insurance status; logistic regression to test associations between these factors and tailored responses.
Bias Definition: Bias is defined as clinically unjustified differences in ChatGPT’s recommendations or tailoring of responses based on patient social factors (race, gender, insurance status).
Conclusions: Insurance status: Yes (clinically inappropriate differences in recommendations; uninsured patients recommended community health clinic while insured patients recommended ED for high-risk chest pain). Race: No. Gender: No.
Other Bias: Yes (insurance status (good insurance vs no insurance))

Title: A large language model-based approach to quantifying the effects of social determinants in liver transplant decisions.
Data Source: Deidentified electronic medical record (EMR) data; psychosocial evaluation notes from liver transplant evaluations at UCSF
Data Details: Each data point is a single adult patient evaluated for liver transplantation at UCSF, represented by their psychosocial evaluation note and linked structured demographic and clinical variables.
Topic: leverage systematically extracted psychosocial and social determinant factors to quantify their influence on liver transplant evaluation outcomes relative to clinical variables and explain demographic disparities through modifiable factors, enabling the identification of specific intervention targets
Note: LLMs can reliably extract psychosocial risk and SDOH factors from clinical notes. Psychosocial risk and SDOH explained 42.6% of racial disparities
Bias Evaluation Metric: Prevalence differences across demographics; Blinder–Oaxaca decomposition of racial listing gaps; regression coefficients for listing vs. social factors
Bias Definition: Bias conceptualized as demographic disparities in transplant recommendation and listing that are explained or not explained by psychosocial risk and SDOH factors
Conclusions: Race/ethnicity: Yes (substantial racial/ethnic disparities in psychosocial factors and listing; SDOH explain much of Asian advantage but not gaps for unknown/undisclosed race). Gender/sex: Yes (different psychosocial risk patterns and barriers by sex). Other SDOH groups (e.g., Indigenous/Pacific Islander, Hispanic/Latino): Yes (higher barriers such as transportation, mental health treatment gaps).
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (Non-Hispanic White, Hispanic or Latino, Asian, Black or African American, Indigenous and Pacific Islanders, Other, Unknown/Declined)
Gender Bias: Yes (The distribution of patient race and ethnicity within the cohort is 42% Non-Hispanic White... with a gender distribution of 37.5% female and 62.5% male.)
Other Bias: Yes (socioeconomic / access / social support / mental health)
Geography / Cultural Bias: Yes (within United States; disparities across racial/ethnic groups in a single U.S. transplant center)

Title: Assessing the diagnostic performance of ChatGPT in dermatology across Fitzpatrick phototypes and skin of color.
Journal: American Academy of Dermatology (J Am Acad Dermatol)
Data Source: 170 dermatology clinical images (85 non–skin of color, 85 skin of color) of 17 common skin diseases; labels confirmed by 3 board‑certified dermatologists
Data Details: Each data point is an image that may consist of 17 common dermatoses; 5 non‑SoC + 5 SoC images per disease; Fitzpatrick V–VI or non‑Caucasian for SoC; images matched on variant, severity, location
Topic: Diagnostic performance and fairness of ChatGPT‑4o across Fitzpatrick phototypes / skin of color vs non‑SoC dermatology images
Note: Overall accuracy high, but markedly worse on skin of color (SoC ≈ 71.8% vs non‑SoC ≈ 90.6%); underrepresentation of SoC images in training likely cause; authors urge cautious clinical use and more diverse datasets
Bias Evaluation Metric: Compare diagnostic performance across Fitzpatrick phototypes
Bias Definition: Diagnostic accuracy differences across skin types
Conclusions: Yes (Performance disparities across Fitzpatrick phototypes indicate bias)
Other Bias: Yes (skin phototype / skin of color vs non‑SoC；also mentions “health care disparities, skin of color” as key words)

Title: Assessment of Medically Relevant Ageism Inherent in Large Language Models.
Data Source: Multiple-choice question dataset about medically related ageism scenarios (constructed text prompts, English + Japanese)
Data Details: Each data point is a multiple-choice question assessing ageism in healthcare, labeled across five ageism dimensions and provided in both English and Japanese.
Topic: Assessment of medically relevant ageism in general-purpose LLMs using a labeled multiple-choice dataset in English and Japanese.
Note: Many LLMs exhibit stereotypical ageism, Almost all LLMs tended to be biased in the direction of stereotypes, Many of the models exhibited an anti-stereotypical tendency in relation to the neglect of the medical complaints.
Bias Evaluation Metric: Kendall’s tau between question stereotype labels and LLM output labels; significance tested at 5% level.
Bias Definition: Bias defined as systematic alignment of LLM choices with age-related stereotypes embedded in the question labels
Conclusions: Older adults: Yes (LLMs show stereotypical ageism, especially reluctance to intervene in treatment and self directed ageism). Younger adults: Mixed / Anti-stereotype (some anti stereotypical tendencies in neglecting medical complaints).

Title: Can AI Bridge or Widen Maternal Health Inequities?
Data Source: None
Data Details: Each data point is a conceptual argument or policy insight drawn from a narrative synthesis of existing literature and policy documents; no primary dataset is used.
Topic: How AI in maternal healthcare can either bridge or widen inequities in LMICs, via a four-domain equity framework.
Note: The framework highlights inclusive data, equitable governance, participatory design, and local capacity.
Bias Evaluation Metric: This commentary employs a narrative synthesis of peer-reviewed literature, WHO technical documentation, and global health policy analyses.
Bias Definition: Bias understood as structural and algorithmic factors that reproduce or exacerbate existing maternal health inequities, especially in LMICs.
Conclusions: LMIC women and marginalized groups: Yes (AI can widen existing inequities if data, governance, design, and local capacity are not equity-oriented). Well resourced contexts: Mixed (AI can help bridge gaps when deployed with inclusive data and participatory, accountable governance).
Other Bias: Yes: socioeconomic status, and geographic location
Geography / Cultural Bias: Yes (focus on global inequities with emphasis on LMICs (especially sub-Saharan Africa and other low/middle-income regions))
Reference Standard (Human / Model / Sy: System (the article uses conceptual and policy frameworks, not human or clinician ratings(

Title: Computational challenges arising in algorithmic fairness and health equity with generative AI.
Data Source: clinical notes、EHR、psych notes、substance use notes
Data Details: Each data point is a conceptual example or discussion related to fairness metrics drawn from prior work; no primary dataset is used.
Topic: generative AI fairness + health equity in healthcare
Note: high-level comment on fairness metrics, training data transparency, and interventions like filtering / model editing
Other Bias: Yes: marginalized and minority groups, social groups, hateful content groups

Title: Evaluating the impact of sex bias on AI models in musculoskeletal ultrasound of joint recess distension.
Data Source: 5,000 de-identified MSKUS knee ultrasound images from community clinics in Ontario, Canada
Data Details: Each data point is an image. It consists of de-identified knee MSKUS images, 2010–2018, adult patients, suprapatellar view, 4 cohorts: male/female × with/without distension, expert sonographer labeled presence/absence
Topic: Sex bias effects on CNN performance for knee synovial recess distension (and sex classification) from ultrasound
Note: Female-only and balanced training outperform male-only; sex classifier 95% accuracy; Grad-CAM shows sex-specific focus areas. Researchers also utilized Grad-CAM to visualize the regions of interest in ultrasound images that contributed most significantly to the model’s predictions
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, sensitivity, AUC, specificity
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics between models trained on only data from one group, seeing which models generalize well.
Conclusions: Female: No, Male: Yes
Debias-details: Researchers compare models that have been trained on only male data, only female data, or both.

Title: Exploring biases related to the use of large language models in a multilingual depression corpus.
Data Source: RADAR-MDD speech recordings (English, Spanish, Dutch) + PHQ‑8, from London / Amsterdam / Barcelona
Data Details: Each data point is a free‑response speech recording from a participant with major depressive disorder in the RADAR‑MDD cohort, paired with its automatically generated text transcription and PHQ‑8 depression severity score.
Topic: demographic (age, gender) and language biases in LLM-based depression severity classification on multilingual speech-derived text
Note: age balance had stronger impact than gender; performance varies across languages; tests multiple LLMs
Bias Evaluation Metric: Unweighted Average Recall (UAR) and F1-score, high symptom severity in vector embeddings
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: Female: Yes, Male: No, English: No, Spanish: Yes, Dutch: No, <40: Yes, 40–50: No, 50–60: Yes, >60: No
Evaluated LLMs: FlanT5, RoBERTa, mBERT (MentalBERT), GPT‑2, BERT (multilingual), LLaMA, MentaLLaMA

Title: Large language models propagate race-based medicine.
Data Source: LLM outputs from ChatGPT-3.5, GPT‑4, Bard, Claude on 9 race-based-medicine questions（45 responses each）
Data Details: Each data point is a single model response to one race-related clinical prompt from a commercial large language model, with nine questions each asked five times per model, yielding 45 outputs per model.
Topic: whether major LLMs regurgitate race-based medicine and racist medical tropes
Note: all four models had runs with race-based medicine; stochastic; some fabricated equations
Bias Evaluation Metric: Number of runs (out of 5 total runs) that had concerning race-based responses
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics, qualitative analysis of the responses
Conclusions: Black: Yes, White: No
Evaluated LLMs: ChatGPT (3.5 versions May 12, Aug 3), GPT‑4, Bard (May 18, Aug 3), Claude (May 15, Aug 3)

Title: Sociodemographic Bias in Large Language Model-Assisted Gastroenterology.
Data Source: 100 synthetic gastroenterology clinic vignettes generated by ChatGPT‑o1, each with 34 sociodemographic versions; LLM outputs to 4 clinical questions
Data Details: Each data point is a combination of the following. They select one of 100 ChatGPT‑o1 generated GI cases. They select one type of demographic information, that being either the control or 33 other sociodemographic versions (race, gender identity, SES, housing, etc.). Then, they select one of 4 clinical questions. Each version was given to 10 LLMs.
Topic: sociodemographic bias (race, gender identity, income, housing, etc.) in LLM recommendations for GI cases, esp. mental health referrals
Note: 10 LLM ensemble; 136k responses; higher odds of mental health referral for transgender, unhoused, low-income, unemployed vignettes
Bias Evaluation Metric: Recommendations for endoscopy, recommendations for mental health interventions
Bias Definition: Statistically significant difference in odds of needing an endoscopy or needing mental health intervention
Conclusions: Low income: No, middle income: No, High income: No, Unhoused: Yes, Housed: No, Black: No, White: No, Asian: No, Hispanic: No, Native American: No, Heterosexual: Yes, Transgender man: Yes, Transgender woman: Yes, Nonbinary: Yes, Gay or Lesbian: No, Bisexual: No, Male: No, Female: No
Race / Ethnic Bias: Yes (Asian, Black, Hispanic, Native American, White)
Other Bias: Yes (low income, middle income, high income, unemployed, unhoused)
LGBTQ+ Bias: Yes (transgender man, transgender woman, nonbinary (they/them), gay/lesbian, bisexual, heterosexual)

Title: Uncovering Language Disparity of ChatGPT on Retinal Vascular Disease Classification: Cross-Sectional Study.
Journal: Journal of Medical Internet Research (J Med Internet Res)
Data Source: 1226 Chinese FFA reports + corresponding clinical diagnoses from one hospital (SAHZU Eye Center)
Data Details: Each data point is a Chinese fundus fluorescein angiography report for a single eye, paired with its gold-standard retinal vascular disease diagnosis from the hospital electronic record.
Topic: language disparity (Chinese vs English prompts) in ChatGPT’s retinal vascular disease diagnosis and reasoning on Chinese FFA reports
Note: direct English prompts best F1=80.05%; Chinese prompts ~6–7% lower; Step vs Direct trade-offs; reasoning eval (hallucinations, misinformation, incompleteness)
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, F1 Score, recall, precision, number of reasoning steps, the number of reasoning errors, and the incompleteness of the inference process, rates of misinformation, rates of information omission, rates of hallucinations, rates of inconsistency, response length in tokens
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in the aforementioned metrics, including a Chi-Squared test
Conclusions: English: No, Chinese: Yes

Title: Quality safety and disparity of an AI chatbot in managing chronic diseases: simulated patient experiments.
Data Source: Simulated patient (SP) scripts for unstable angina and asthma + ERNIE Bot / ChatGPT‑4o / DeepSeek‑R1 responses
Data Details: Each data point is a simulated patient–AI consultation trial in which a trained standardized patient presents an unstable angina or asthma case with a predefined sociodemographic profile to ERNIE Bot, and the full dialogue and management plan are recorded for evaluation.
Topic: 384 ERNIE Bot sessions + 40 ChatGPT‑4o + 40 DeepSeek‑R1quality, safety, and sociodemographic disparity in AI chatbot management of chronic diseases via simulated patients
Note: ERNIE: diagnostic accuracy 77.3%, correct medication 94.3%, unnecessary tests 91.9%, unnecessary meds 57.8%; more intensive care for older/wealthier SPs; ChatGPT‑4o / DeepSeek R1 even higher accuracy but more overprescribing
Bias Evaluation Metric: Accuracy, rates of requesting unnecessary lab tests, rates of prescribing inappropriate or even potentially harmful medications
Bias Definition: Statistically significant differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: 65 Years: Yes, 55 Years: No, Woman: No, Man: No, Poor: Yes, Rich: No, Urban Employee Medical Insurance: Yes, Urban and Rural Resident Medical Insurance: No, Urban: No, Non‑urban: No
Other Bias: Household economic status (poor, rich), Health insurance (Urban Employee Medical Insurance, Urban and Rural Resident Medical Insurance), Residence (urban, non‑urban)

Title: Beyond Tokens: Fair Evaluation of French Large Language Models for Clinical Named Entity Recognition.
Journal: Volume 316: Digital Health and Informatics Innovations for Sustainable Health Care Systems
Data Source: CANTEMIST‑FR subset of FRASIMED: 1301 synthetic French oncological reports with morphology entities
Data Language: French (CANTEMIST‑FR); Spanish used only as source for projection
Data Details: Each data point is a synthetic French oncological clinical note from the CANTEMIST‑FR corpus annotated with one or more morphological oncology entities for named entity recognition
Topic: clinical French NER for oncology morphologies; comparison of token‑ vs entity‑level metrics; BERT vs ChatGPT prompting
Note: CamemBERT best token‑level F1, FlauBERT best entity‑level; DrBERT many PARTIALs; ChatGPT has low recall but decent relaxed precision
Bias Evaluation Metric: Precision, Recall, F1-Score
Evaluated LLMs: CamemBERT (Base), FlauBERT (Base), CamemBERT‑Bio (Base), DrBERT (Base), ChatGPT (GPT‑3.5, zero‑shot and few‑shot)

Title: Beyond the Rubric: Cultural Misalignment in LLM Benchmarks for Sexual and Reproductive Health.
Journal: Proceedings of the 5th Workshop on Evaluation and Comparison of NLP Systems
Data Source: Subset of HealthBench conversations filtered for SRH (637 queries, 330 single‑turn used), Responses from Myna Bolo SRH chatbot (RAG + intent detection, WhatsApp)
Data Details: Each data point is a question related to sexual and reproductive health. Of the 637 questions, 330 where single‑turn used; 244 were answered by Myna Bolo and auto‑graded.
Topic: Researchers studied cultural misalignment and Western bias in HealthBench’s automated rubric when evaluating SRH chatbot for Global South (India)
Note: HealthBench rubric often assumes US laws, diet, insurance; penalizes India‑specific, concise, patient‑centric answers judged safe by local experts; authors propose culturally adaptive, region‑aware evaluation layers
Bias Evaluation Metric: Qualitative analysis of criteria like "Legality of Breastfeeding in Public", "Locality of Pregnancy Diet", "Regionality of System of Care and Financing for IUD and IVF", "Bias towards US Prescriptions and Resources for Postpartum Depression", "Conceptions of 'Normal' in Period Delay", "Query Ambiguity (age vs duration) in Heavy Bleeding"
Bias Definition: Qualitatively evaluated preference or usage or cultural norms or information from the West or USA, rather than India.
Conclusions: USA: No, India: Yes

Title: Demographic Bias of Expert-Level Vision-Language Foundation Models in Medical Imaging.
Data Source: Experiment on Existing ModelsFive public chest x‑ray datasets: MIMIC-CXR, CheXpert, NIH ChestX-ray14, PadChest, VinDr
Data Details: Each data point may come from Chest x‑ray datasets, number of images / patients, demographics (sex, age, race), some with radiologist annotations; resized and normalized before evaluation. It comes from the following: MIMIC 357,167; CheXpert 223,458; NIH 112,120; PadChest 160,736; VinDr 5,323 images.
Topic: Bias and fairness of chest x‑ray vision‑language foundation models vs radiologists
Note: Model underdiagnoses marginalized and intersectional groups; encodes demographic info strongly
Bias Evaluation Metric: True-Positive Rates (TPR), True-Negative Rates (TNR), ROC Curves, and AUC
Bias Definition: Differences in the aforementioned metrics
Conclusions: Female: Yes, Male: No, Black: Yes, White: No, Asian, No, Other race: No, 0-18: Yes, 18-40: Yes, 40-60: No, 60-80: No, >80: No
Evaluated LLMs: CheXzero, Knowledge-enhanced Auto Diagnosis (KAD), supervised DenseNet baseline
